sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=19, col=48).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 17, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking on objects (yellow dot, blue square) only changes the timer, not gameplay. Arrow keys move the green dot within its container. The green dot seems to be the only interactive element but is trapped.
- Goal model: Still unclear. Need to find the actual game mechanic.
- Action model: Arrow keys move green dot within container. MOUSE clicks on objects don't seem to do anything meaningful.
- Plan: Try clicking on the divider or gaps to see if that triggers something. Also try clicking on the right panel shapes.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 16 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
BIG DISCOVERY! Clicking on the red shape on the right panel caused major changes:
1. The red shape outline (white pixels) on the right panel DISAPPEARED - all W pixels turned to B (black)
2. A new red rectangle appeared on the left panel at rows 24-29, cols 18-21 (4x6 red block)
3. The red bar (Node 14) on the left panel DISAPPEARED - R pixels at rows 30-32, cols 12-17 turned to c (charcoal)
So clicking on the red shape on the right:
- Removed the red shape's outline on the right
- Moved the red bar from the left to a new position on the left (rows 24-29, cols 18-21)
- The red bar disappeared from its original position
Wait, let me re-read. The red bar was at rows 30-32, cols 12-17. It disappeared (turned to c). And a new red block appeared at rows 24-29, cols 18-21.
So the mechanic seems to be:
- Clicking on a colored shape on the right panel "activates" it
- This moves/transfers the corresponding colored object from the left panel to a new position
- The outline on the right disappears after clicking
Let me look at the current state to understand better. The red shape outline is gone, and a red block appeared. The blue shape outline is still there.
Maybe the goal is to click on all the shapes on the right to "solve" them, and each click moves the corresponding colored object to fill the shape's position.
Let me check the current state and try clicking on the blue shape.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: BREAKTHROUGH! Clicking on the red shape (right panel) caused: (1) red outline disappeared on right, (2) red bar disappeared from left (rows 30-32, cols 12-17), (3) new red block appeared at rows 24-29, cols 18-21. The game mechanic is: clicking shapes on the right panel transfers/moves corresponding colored objects on the left.
Goal model: Click on all shapes on the right panel to solve the puzzle. Each click moves the matching colored object from the left.
Action model: MOUSE clicks on right-panel shapes activate them and move corresponding left-panel objects.
Plan: Click on the blue shape on the right panel to continue solving.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after red shape click
seg = current_frame.segmentation
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# List current objects
print("Current objects (non-background):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ('c', 'B', 'G', 'W', 'g'):
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check right panel area
print("\nRight panel (rows 15-40, cols 35-55):")
for r in range(15, 40):
    print(f"  Row {r}: {ascii_lines[r][35:55]}")

# Check left panel area
print("\nLeft panel (rows 20-35, cols 10-25):")
for r in range(20, 35):
    print(f"  Row {r}: {ascii_lines[r][10:25]}")

# Click on the blue shape on the right (center ~36, 48)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 48}])
print(f"\nAfter clicking blue shape: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

# Check what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame
before_ascii = before.ascii.split('\n')
after_ascii = after.ascii.split('\n')
diff_rows = [i for i in range(len(before_ascii)) if before_ascii[i] != after_ascii[i] and i != 63]
print(f"Changed rows (excluding timer): {diff_rows}")
for r in sorted(diff_rows):
    b_row = before_ascii[r]
    a_row = after_ascii[r]
    diffs = [(i, b_row[i], a_row[i]) for i in range(len(b_row)) if b_row[i] != a_row[i]]
    print(f"  Row {r}: {diffs[:10]}{'...' if len(diffs)>10 else ''}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
